focus: correctness: error handling & resource cleanup (timeouts, aborts, child processes)

title: Immediate judge rejection leaves fast-timeout timer alive
detail: callJudgeWithDualTimeouts starts invoke(), schedules fastTimer, and awaits the first Promise.race. clearTimeout(fastTimer) only runs after that race fulfills, so an immediate invoke rejection jumps to runJudgeWithHardening's catch path while the fast timer remains scheduled until fastTimeoutMs, which defaults to 10000ms. Repeated fast model errors can keep the event loop open and accumulate idle timers.
claim: Immediate model errors leak the fast timeout timer until it fires.
counterevidenceSought: Checked for a finally block or rejection cleanup around the first race and for tests asserting no pending timers after a model_error retry. The cleanup exists only after a fulfilled race, and the tests cover retry/slow-path behavior without timer-leak assertions.
alternativeExplanation: Short test timeouts can mask the leak because the timer eventually fires; production defaults make the retained timer observable as process tail latency.
downgradeTrigger: Downgrade if every invoke implementation is proven never to reject after fastTimer is scheduled, or if callers always tear down the process before pending timers can retain the event loop.
suggestedFix: Wrap the first Promise.race in try/finally and clear fastTimer in the finally path on both fulfillment and rejection; preserve the existing slow-path operation.catch suppression for operations that outlive slowTimeoutMs.
